entities.label: Metal movable-type printing (Europe)
entities.shortDescription: The European system for printing from mass-produced, reusable cast-metal type, assembled by Johannes Gutenberg in Mainz by 1454-1455.
entities.fullDescription: Between roughly 1436 and 1455 Johannes Gutenberg combined pre-existing technologies (the screw press, oil-based ink, the concept of movable type) with an adjustable hand mould for casting uniform metal type into a repeatable system for mass-producing books. Ceramic, wooden and metal movable type existed earlier in China and Korea; Gutenberg's decisive contribution was the casting system and integrated press-and-ink workflow suited to a small alphabet. By 1500 presses operated in over 200 European towns.

entities.label: Johannes Gutenberg
entities.shortDescription: Mainz goldsmith (c. 1400-1468) who developed the European metal movable-type printing system and produced the 42-line Bible.

entities.label: Johann Fust
entities.shortDescription: Mainz financier (d. 1466); lent Gutenberg two ~800-guilder sums, sued him in 1455/56 and won control of the Bible workshop.

entities.label: Peter Schöffer
entities.shortDescription: Former Paris calligrapher (c. 1425-1503); typographer for Gutenberg and Fust; co-produced the 1457 Mainz Psalter.

entities.label: Adjustable hand mould
entities.shortDescription: Gutenberg's device for casting large numbers of identical metal letters quickly from a punched matrix; widely called his single most ingenious invention.

entities.label: Screw press
entities.shortDescription: Pre-existing agricultural press (wine/oil) whose mechanism Gutenberg adapted for printing; he re-engineered the platen, not the press itself.

entities.label: Gutenberg Bible (42-line Bible)
entities.shortDescription: First major European book printed with mass-produced metal movable type; Mainz, completed 1454-1455.

entities.label: Mainz Psalter
entities.shortDescription: First dated printed book, with the first colophon and printed red/blue initials; Fust & Schöffer, Mainz, August 1457.

entities.label: Jikji
entities.shortDescription: Korean Buddhist anthology (1377), the world's oldest extant book printed with metal movable type - predating Gutenberg by decades.

entities.label: Laurens Janszoon Coster
entities.shortDescription: Haarlem figure said (from a c. 1567 account) to have printed in the 1420s; no known works survive and the connection is today regarded as legend.

entities.label: Prokop Waldvogel
entities.shortDescription: Avignon silversmith with 1444-1446 contracts for 'artificial writing' referencing metal type-like equipment; no printed books are known from him.

entities.label: William Caxton
entities.shortDescription: Merchant-printer who set up the first press in England at Westminster in 1476 after printing at Bruges with Colard Mansion.

entities.label: Mainz
entities.shortDescription: Rhineland city where Gutenberg developed his press and printed the 42-line Bible; its 1462 sack dispersed trained printers across Europe.

chronology.label: Gutenberg develops the system (date contested: 1436/1439/1440/c.1450)
chronology.note: Anchor dates conflict across sources; earliest hard evidence is the Dec 1439 Dritzehn lawsuit testimony. See disputed-claims question.

chronology.label: Bible pages shown at Frankfurt (reported by Piccolomini, March 1455)
Spread from Mainz to 200+ European towns
Gutenberg born, Mainz (year unknown; 1400 assigned by convention)
Mainz Psalter printed by Fust & Schöffer
Jikji printed with metal movable type, Korea
Caxton establishes the first English press at Westminster

connections.explanation: Gutenberg assembled the system (casting, ink, press workflow); movable type as a concept predates him in Asia.
The adjustable hand mould is Gutenberg's original, decisive contribution (exact casting method contested - see question).
The hand mould is the component that made mass production of uniform type possible.
The press mechanism was adapted from ubiquitous agricultural screw presses; Gutenberg re-engineered the platen.
Two ~800-guilder loans (c. 1449-50 and 1452) financed the press and the Bible work.
Fust sued for repayment in 1455/56; the court sided with Fust, who took control of the Bible workshop.
Produced by Fust & Schöffer after the workshop passed to Fust.
Ran the first commercially successful printing house together; Schöffer was Fust's son-in-law.
Korean metal movable type (Jikji, 1377) predates the European system by decades.
CONTESTED: Gies argues westward transmission is 'almost certain'; McDermott counters that no evidence of Asian movable type in Europe before 1450 exists. Held for human review.
Legendary rival-inventor claim (Haarlem, 1420s) from Junius's Batavia (c. 1567); 'today regarded as a mere legend'; no known works.
1444-46 Avignon contracts reference metal type-like equipment ('ars artificialiter scribendi'), but no printed books; priority rests with Gutenberg on surviving evidence.
Caxton brought the technology to England (Westminster, 1476) after printing at Bruges.
Developed and first commercially proven in Mainz (Humbrechthof workshop; 42-line Bible).

sources.title: Wikipedia: Global spread of the printing press
History of Information: Gutenberg Begins Experimentation on Printing (1439 Strasbourg lawsuit)
Prepressure: 1400-1499, history of printing in the 15th century
Dritzehn heirs v. Gutenberg - Strasbourg court testimony, 12 Dec 1439 (referenced via secondary sources)
Enea Silvio Piccolomini (later Pius II), letter reporting Bible pages seen at Frankfurt, c. March 1455 (referenced via secondary sources)

claims.text: The 42-line Bible (Mainz, completed 1454-1455) is the first major European book printed with mass-produced metal movable type.
Testimony in the December 1439 Dritzehn lawsuit records a press, molten lead, forms and punches - the earliest hard evidence of Gutenberg's printing experiments.

sourceLinks.quotation: the court records refer to purchases of lead and printing materials - to forms and pieces that make sense only in terms of printing with movable type.

claims.text: The future Pope Pius II reported seeing pages of the Bible displayed at Frankfurt in a letter of March 1455 - the key external dating evidence.
Fust lent Gutenberg two sums of about 800 guilders, sued for repayment in 1455/56, and was awarded control of the Bible workshop, leaving Gutenberg effectively bankrupt.
The adjustable hand mould with punched matrix allowed large numbers of identical, precisely sized metal letters to be cast quickly, and is widely called Gutenberg's single most ingenious invention.
The Mainz Psalter (August 1457) is the first printed book bearing a date and colophon, with printed red and blue initials.
The Jikji (Korea, 1377) is the world's oldest extant book printed with metal movable type, predating Gutenberg's work by decades.

sourceLinks.quotation: the world's oldest metallic movable type-printed book

claims.text: Movable type stayed marginal in East Asia partly because logographic scripts require thousands of characters; Europe's small alphabet made a complete reusable type set economically viable.

Historians dispute whether Gutenberg invented independently of East Asian movable type: Gies calls westward transmission 'almost certain'; McDermott finds no evidence of Asian movable type in Europe before 1450.

sourceLinks.quotation: No text indicates the presence or knowledge of any kind of Asian movable type or movable type imprint in Europe before 1450.

claims.text: By 1500 presses operated in more than 200 European towns (sources give 236, ~250 or ~270); total incunabula output is estimated between roughly 8 and 20 million volumes across some 30,000-40,000 editions - figures disputed.
Scholars estimate 160-185 Bible copies were printed (a 1455 letter cites both 158 and 180); 49 were known to survive as of 2009, only 25 complete.

The claim that Coster invented printing in Haarlem in the 1420s rests on Junius's Batavia (written c. 1567); no works printed by him are known, and the connection 'is today regarded as a mere legend'.

sourceLinks.quotation: The Coster connection is today regarded as a mere legend.

media.alt: Placeholder plate for metal movable-type printing; no archival image asserted.

questions.detail: When did Gutenberg actually invent printing? Sources anchor on 1436 (partnership), 1439 (lawsuit evidence), 1440 ('perfected the secret'), or c. 1450-55 (Bible work). Chronology entry t-invented uses a low-confidence range.
How was Gutenberg's type cast? Punch-and-matrix vs a more 'primitive' method is debated; 2001 research found his earliest type shows variations inconsistent with pure punch-matrix casting.

Asian priority is established (Jikji 1377, Bi Sheng c. 1040, Wang Zhen c. 1298), but whether the technique or a report of it reached Europe is contested (Gies vs McDermott). Connection rel-asia is asserted only as low-confidence interpretation.
Output figures conflict: 8M vs 20M volumes by 1500; 220 presses vs 236/250/270 towns; press rate 3,200-3,600 impressions/day vs 240/hour. Magnitude agreed, exact figures vary by source.
Britannica could not be machine-fetched (HTTP 403) during research; its dates are consistent with other sources but should be confirmed by hand before any public use.

nextEntities.title: Aldus Manutius
nextEntities.reason: Venice; octavo format and italic type (1499-1501) - the next transformation of the printed book.

nextEntities.reason: Long-run consequences (literacy, Reformation, Scientific Revolution) need their own carefully-classed records - influence, not direct causation.
